subtopic_detail: New atrial fibrillation rate control and anticoagulation
stem:
A 73-year-old woman attends your general practice clinic with 3 days of palpitations and exertional breathlessness. She says 'my heart has been racing on and off, and the chemist found my pulse was all over the shop'. She has a history of hypertension treated with amlodipine, type 2 diabetes treated with metformin, and previous transient ischaemic attack 5 years ago that was managed conservatively. She has no chest pain, no syncope, and no symptoms suggesting heart failure.

On examination her pulse is irregularly irregular at 124 bpm. Heart sounds are dual with no murmurs. Chest is clear. There is no peripheral oedema. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 124 bpm, no ischaemic ST changes, and QRS 90 ms. TSH 2.0 mIU/L; electrolytes, renal and liver function within normal limits; haemoglobin 132 g/L. Bedside echocardiogram shows preserved left ventricular function with no significant valvular disease. CHA2DS2-VA score is calculated.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124 irregularly irregular, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Irregularly irregular pulse, no murmur, no signs of heart failure, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: ECG atrial fibrillation HR 124 bpm, no ST changes; TSH 2.0 mIU/L; UEC and LFTs within normal limits; haemoglobin 132 g/L; bedside echo preserved LV function, no significant valvular disease
data_table.Medications: Amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1 g twice daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Electrical cardioversion this admission and discharge home on aspirin alone for the long-term stroke prophylaxis
options.rationale: Aspirin is not adequate stroke prophylaxis in atrial fibrillation; CHA2DS2-VA score over 1 in this patient (age, hypertension, diabetes, prior TIA) mandates oral anticoagulation. Cardioversion in unscreened AF over 48 hours requires either transoesophageal echo to exclude thrombus or 3-4 weeks of anticoagulation first. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence intravenous heparin infusion and refer for transoesophageal-guided cardioversion within 24 hours
options.rationale: TOE-guided cardioversion is a reasonable strategy when prompt rhythm control is needed, but rate control with oral anticoagulation is the AU first-line for newly diagnosed AF of uncertain duration; rhythm strategy is decided at outpatient follow-up. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Refer urgently for pulmonary vein isolation ablation procedure and commence aspirin pending the catheter laboratory date
options.rationale: Ablation is reserved for symptomatic paroxysmal or persistent AF refractory to medical therapy; first-line is rate or rhythm control and oral anticoagulation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Add bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily for rhythm control and arrange repeat ECG and routine review in 6 weeks without other therapy
options.rationale: Bisoprolol is for rate control, not rhythm control; anticoagulation is missing from this option and is the priority for stroke prevention. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline)

options.text: Commence apixaban 5 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily for rate control, and refer cardiology for outpatient rhythm decision
options.rationale: CHA2DS2-VA score of 5 (age, hypertension, diabetes, prior TIA - the VA scheme drops sex; under AU practice this score mandates anticoagulation). Apixaban is AU first-line DOAC, well-tolerated, no INR monitoring required. Bisoprolol provides rate control. Cardiology decides on rhythm vs rate strategy at outpatient review; if rhythm control is desired and AF is over 48 hours, anticoagulation precedes cardioversion. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ AF Guideline; AMH)

explanation.summary: New atrial fibrillation is managed with stroke prophylaxis (CHA2DS2-VA-based anticoagulation) and either rate or rhythm control. Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are AU first-line; warfarin is reserved for mechanical valves or moderate-severe mitral stenosis. Rate control (beta-blocker first, calcium channel blocker if beta-blocker contraindicated) targets resting heart rate under 110 bpm (lenient) or 80 bpm (strict). Cardioversion in unscreened AF over 48 hours requires either TOE-guided or 3-4 weeks of anticoagulation first.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VA replaces CHA2DS2-VASc in AU - drops sex points
DOAC first-line; warfarin only for mechanical valve / moderate-severe MS
Rate control: bisoprolol first, target HR under 110 bpm
Cardioversion in AF over 48 hours: TOE-guided or 3-4 weeks anticoagulation first
explanation.pearls: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily is the AU first-line DOAC for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; reduce to 2.5 mg twice daily if any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are present.

stem:
A 54-year-old woman is brought to your emergency department resuscitation bay by ambulance with sudden-onset dyspnoea and pre-syncope. Her partner says 'she just stood up after the long flight home this morning and went pale and dizzy'. She had a knee arthroplasty 3 weeks ago. She has no history of bleeding, no stroke, no recent surgery within 2 weeks, and no malignancy. She is currently taking enoxaparin prophylactic dose at home.

On examination she is dyspnoeic. There is no leg swelling. JVP is raised at 7 cm. Cardiac auscultation reveals a loud P2 and right ventricular heave. Chest is clear. Bedside echocardiogram shows right ventricular dilatation with septal flattening and a McConnell sign of preserved apical contractility with akinetic basal-mid right ventricular free wall. ECG shows sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3 pattern, and right axis deviation. A point-of-care CT pulmonary angiogram, completed within 10 minutes of arrival, confirms saddle pulmonary embolus.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132, BP 84/56 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 88% on room air rising to 94% on 10 L oxygen, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Dyspnoeic, JVP 7 cm, loud P2, right ventricular heave, no leg swelling, no calf tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Bedside echo: RV dilatation, septal flattening, McConnell sign; ECG sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3, right axis deviation; CTPA saddle PE; troponin 0.4 microgram/L (under 0.04); haemoglobin 128 g/L; platelets 220 x10^9/L; INR 1.0

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Administer intravenous alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours as systemic thrombolysis for haemodynamically unstable PE
options.rationale: Haemodynamically unstable PE (systolic BP under 90 mmHg or vasopressor requirement) with confirmed embolus and no absolute contraindication is treated with systemic thrombolysis. The AU regimen is alteplase 100 mg intravenously over 2 hours, then anticoagulation. Reduced dose or catheter-directed thrombolysis is considered in high bleeding-risk patients. Surgical embolectomy is reserved for failed thrombolysis or absolute contraindications. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ VTE Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Commence intravenous unfractionated heparin infusion at therapeutic rate and admit to the high-dependency unit
options.rationale: Heparin alone is the standard for haemodynamically stable PE; with shock (systolic BP under 90 mmHg), thrombolysis or thrombectomy is the AU intervention; heparin is given alongside but is not sufficient on its own. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ VTE Guideline)

options.text: Refer for inferior vena cava filter placement before any antithrombotic therapy is administered to this patient
options.rationale: IVC filters are reserved for absolute contraindication to anticoagulation or recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation; they do not treat the embolus already in situ. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ VTE Guideline)

options.text: Increase enoxaparin to 1 mg/kg subcutaneously twice daily and admit for haemodynamic monitoring overnight
options.rationale: Therapeutic-dose LMWH is appropriate for haemodynamically stable PE; shock features (hypotension, low SpO2 needing oxygen) place this in the high-risk category requiring thrombolysis. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ VTE Guideline)

options.text: Trial fluid resuscitation alone with 2 L normal saline and reassess haemodynamic and respiratory status in 60 minutes
options.rationale: Aggressive fluid loading in obstructive shock from PE can worsen right ventricular overload and reduce cardiac output; small judicious volume is acceptable while reperfusion therapy is given, not as primary treatment. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ VTE Guideline)

explanation.summary: Pulmonary embolism is risk-stratified by haemodynamic status, right ventricular strain, and cardiac biomarker elevation. High-risk (massive) PE - shock or sustained hypotension - is treated with systemic thrombolysis unless absolute contraindication, in which case catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical embolectomy is considered. Intermediate-risk PE - normotensive with RV strain - is treated with anticoagulation alone, with close monitoring; rescue thrombolysis if deterioration occurs. Low-risk PE is treated outpatient with DOAC in selected patients.
key_points: Massive PE (shock): systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours
Intermediate-risk PE: anticoagulation, monitor for deterioration
Avoid fluid overload in RV failure; small judicious bolus only
Catheter-directed lysis or surgical embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated
explanation.pearls: Alteplase 100 mg intravenously over 2 hours is the AU thrombolytic regimen for massive PE; followed by unfractionated heparin infusion once thrombin time or APTT confirms adequate clearance.

stem:
A 47-year-old woman is brought to your emergency department by her partner after 3 days of vomiting, abdominal pain, and increasing weakness. He says 'she's been getting darker on her hands and her gums all winter, but she didn't want to bother the doctor'. She has no past psychiatric history. She has been losing weight, feeling weak, and has had postural symptoms. She has no diarrhoea, no fever, and no recent travel.

On examination she is drowsy but rousable. She has hyperpigmentation in her palmar creases and gums, with skin tanning over light-exposed areas. There is no goitre. Her abdomen is soft, mildly tender, and there are no signs of peritonism. Bloods show sodium 122 mmol/L (135-145), potassium 6.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L, glucose 3.0 mmol/L (3.5-6.0), and pH 7.30 on a venous blood gas with bicarbonate 19 mmol/L. She has had no morning cortisol or ACTH check before. Septic screen is sent.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124, BP 84/52 mmHg supine and 70/40 mmHg standing, RR 22, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, hyperpigmentation palmar creases and buccal mucosa, postural drop, soft mildly tender abdomen, no focal signs
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (135-145), K 6.0 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), urea 12 mmol/L, creatinine 110 micromol/L, glucose 3.0 mmol/L (3.5-6.0), VBG pH 7.30, HCO3 19 mmol/L; FBC, septic screen, paired random cortisol and ACTH samples sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Wait for the morning cortisol level to return before any glucocorticoid is administered to confirm diagnosis first
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is life-threatening and treatment must not wait for biochemical confirmation; the paired sample can be drawn before steroid administration but treatment proceeds. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; AMH)

options.text: Commence intravenous dexamethasone 4 mg as the immediate steroid choice, followed by intravenous saline resuscitation
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid effect (important in primary adrenal failure) and is less preferred in established crisis where mineralocorticoid replacement is needed; hydrocortisone is the AU first-line. Dexamethasone is acceptable only when needing to preserve cortisol assay for short ACTH stimulation. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Administer intravenous 50% dextrose 50 mL for the hypoglycaemia and review the haemodynamics after that single bolus
options.rationale: Glucose correction alone does not address adrenal failure and the patient remains in crisis; steroid is the priority intervention alongside fluid and glucose. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat with intravenous normal saline resuscitation and glucose correction
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis (hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, hypotension, hyperpigmentation suggesting primary adrenal failure) is treated empirically with intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, followed by 50-100 mg every 6 hours, alongside isotonic saline resuscitation and glucose correction. Paired cortisol and ACTH can be drawn before the first dose. Mineralocorticoid is not needed acutely at hydrocortisone doses of 50 mg or more (sufficient mineralocorticoid activity). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; AMH)

options.text: Refer urgently for adrenalectomy after CT imaging today and arrange intensive care admission before any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: Surgical referral is irrelevant in acute adrenal crisis from primary adrenal failure; immediate steroid is required. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a medical emergency. Empirical hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenously stat, followed by 50-100 mg every 6 hours, is the AU first-line - given before biochemical confirmation when crisis is clinically suspected. Paired cortisol and ACTH samples can be drawn beforehand for later interpretation. Aggressive isotonic saline (1-2 L initially) and glucose correction address hypovolaemia and hypoglycaemia. Hyperpigmentation in light-exposed areas plus hyperkalaemia and hyponatraemia points to primary adrenal failure (Addison disease); secondary adrenal insufficiency lacks hyperpigmentation and hyperkalaemia.
key_points: Treat first, confirm second - hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat
Paired cortisol and ACTH before first dose if feasible
Hyperpigmentation + hyperkalaemia = primary (Addison); think autoimmune adrenalitis in AU
Sick-day rules: triple hydrocortisone dose for febrile illness or major stress
explanation.pearls: Hydrocortisone 100 mg intravenously stat then 50-100 mg every 6 hours covers both glucocorticoid and adequate mineralocorticoid receptor activity in acute crisis; fludrocortisone is added on transition to maintenance oral therapy.

stem:
A 22-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus presents to your emergency department with 24 hours of nausea, vomiting, and breathlessness. She says 'my insulin pump alarmed yesterday afternoon, and I thought I'd just put a new line in this morning, but I haven't been able to eat'. She has no chest pain, no infection symptoms, and no recent surgery. She is alert and oriented.

On examination she has Kussmaul breathing and dry mucous membranes. There is no focal neurological deficit. Bloods show glucose 24 mmol/L, ketones 5.4 mmol/L (under 0.6), pH 7.18, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), corrected sodium 138 mmol/L, anion gap 24, creatinine 105 micromol/L, urea 8 mmol/L. ECG sinus tachycardia 116 bpm, no T-wave changes. Septic screen sent; chest X-ray clear. Urinary HCG negative. She has had no insulin in 18 hours since the pump failure.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 28 with Kussmaul pattern, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Kussmaul breathing, dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, soft non-tender abdomen, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 24 mmol/L, ketones 5.4 mmol/L (under 0.6), pH 7.18, HCO3 8 mmol/L, Na 134 mmol/L, K 4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), corrected Na 138 mmol/L, anion gap 24, creatinine 105 micromol/L, urea 8 mmol/L; ECG sinus tachycardia, no T-wave changes; CXR clear; urinary HCG negative
data_table.Medications: Continuous subcutaneous insulin pump (failed 18 hours ago)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first intervention?

options.text: Intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 unit/kg/hour as a single first intervention prior to fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Starting insulin before adequate fluid resuscitation worsens hypovolaemia (osmotic diuresis is the primary driver of dehydration) and can precipitate hypoglycaemia and hypokalaemia faster; AU pathways open with fluid first then insulin within the first hour. (Source: ADS/ADIPS DKA Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over the first hour while preparing insulin infusion and potassium replacement
options.rationale: Adult DKA management opens with intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over the first hour to restore intravascular volume. Insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/hour is started within the first hour once potassium is known (over 3.5 mmol/L can give insulin without potassium replacement first; under 3.5 mmol/L withhold insulin and replace potassium). Potassium is added to the second litre of fluid unless K is over 5.5 mmol/L. Add 5% dextrose when glucose reaches 14 mmol/L while continuing insulin to clear ketones. (Source: ADS/ADIPS DKA Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin 0.3 unit/kg bolus immediately and arrange admission to the high-dependency unit
options.rationale: Subcutaneous insulin in established DKA is unreliable due to peripheral vasoconstriction; intravenous infusion is the AU first-line. (Source: ADS/ADIPS DKA Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous 8.4% sodium bicarbonate 50 mL stat to correct the venous acidaemia she has on the gas today
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for pH under 6.9 with shock or hyperkalaemia-related cardiac instability; routine use in DKA worsens intracellular acidosis and prolongs ketosis. (Source: ADS/ADIPS DKA Guideline)

options.text: Empirical intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily as antibiotic cover given the prevalence of infection as a DKA trigger
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not part of the DKA bundle unless infection is clinically suspected; the trigger here is pump failure. (Source: ADS/ADIPS DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Diabetic ketoacidosis in adults is treated with fluid first, insulin second, and aggressive potassium replacement. Open with intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L over the first hour. Start insulin infusion at 0.1 unit/kg/hour once potassium is known; withhold if under 3.5 mmol/L and replace potassium first. Add 5% dextrose to the fluid when glucose falls to around 14 mmol/L (continue insulin until ketones cleared). Treat the precipitant and convert to subcutaneous insulin only when acidosis and ketones have resolved and the patient can eat.
key_points: Fluid first, insulin within the first hour, potassium replacement priority
Withhold insulin if potassium under 3.5 mmol/L until replaced
Add 5% dextrose when glucose under 14 mmol/L while continuing insulin to clear ketones
Bicarbonate reserved for pH under 6.9 with shock or hyperkalaemic instability
explanation.pearls: AU DKA pathway: insulin infusion 0.1 unit/kg/hour without bolus; the priority is volume restoration and potassium replacement, not rapid glucose correction.

stem:
A 78-year-old man presents to your emergency department with sudden right-sided weakness and headache that started 60 minutes ago. He has atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and a mechanical mitral valve replacement 5 years ago. He says 'the room started spinning, then I couldn't lift the right arm at all'. His regular medications are warfarin (target INR 2.5-3.5 for the mitral mechanical valve), bisoprolol, and ramipril. His most recent INR 1 week ago was 2.8.

On examination GCS is 14 (E4 V4 M6). Right-sided hemiparesis power 1/5 upper and 2/5 lower, with right facial droop and dysarthria. Non-contrast CT head shows a 25 mL left basal ganglia haemorrhage with no midline shift and no intraventricular extension. INR returns at 7.0. Platelet count 180 x10^9/L. There is no clinical evidence of mechanical valve thrombosis, no fever, and no recent infection. He has had no falls or trauma. He has had no procedures or surgery recently.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 178/96 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6), right hemiparesis power 1/5 arm and 2/5 leg, right facial droop, dysarthria, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Non-contrast CT head 25 mL left basal ganglia haemorrhage, no midline shift, no intraventricular extension; INR 7.0; platelets 180 x10^9/L; haemoglobin 138 g/L; UEC normal; troponin negative
data_table.Medications: Warfarin (target INR 2.5-3.5), bisoprolol 5 mg daily, ramipril 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate haemostatic intervention?

options.text: Administer fresh frozen plasma 4 units intravenously as the only haemostatic intervention for the elevated INR today
options.rationale: Fresh frozen plasma reverses warfarin slowly (large-volume infusion limits rate), provides incomplete factor replacement, and risks transfusion-associated circulatory overload in the elderly; AU guidelines prefer prothrombin complex concentrate. (Source: NBA Warfarin Reversal Consensus; HSANZ)

options.text: Administer intravenous vitamin K 10 mg as the only intervention given the high INR and the clear ICH on imaging today
options.rationale: Intravenous vitamin K alone takes 6 to 12 hours for full effect, far too slow for ongoing intracerebral haemorrhage; rapid factor replacement is required first. (Source: NBA Warfarin Reversal Consensus)

options.text: Withhold warfarin alone and recheck the INR in 6 hours before administering any further haemostatic interventions then
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in major bleeding with INR over 4 is contraindicated; rapid reversal is the AU standard. (Source: NBA Warfarin Reversal Consensus)

options.text: Administer recombinant activated factor VII 90 micrograms/kg intravenously as the immediate haemostatic intervention
options.rationale: Recombinant factor VIIa is not the AU first-line for warfarin-associated ICH and carries arterial thrombosis risk; prothrombin complex concentrate is preferred for warfarin reversal. (Source: NBA Warfarin Reversal Consensus)

options.text: Administer intravenous prothrombin complex concentrate 50 IU/kg plus intravenous vitamin K 10 mg as the immediate combined reversal
options.rationale: Warfarin-associated major bleeding (intracerebral haemorrhage is the highest-risk category) is reversed with three- or four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (Prothrombinex-VF in AU) at 50 IU/kg plus intravenous vitamin K 10 mg. Prothrombinex restores factors II, IX, and X within minutes; vitamin K provides sustained factor synthesis to prevent rebound coagulopathy as the prothrombin complex concentrate clears. Mechanical mitral valve thrombosis risk in the short term is acceptable in the context of active ICH; restart anticoagulation when imaging stabilises (typically 1 to 4 weeks). (Source: NBA Warfarin Reversal Consensus; HSANZ; ANZSBT)

explanation.summary: Warfarin-associated major bleeding (intracerebral haemorrhage, ongoing major bleeding, or pre-emergency surgery) requires rapid reversal with prothrombin complex concentrate plus intravenous vitamin K. The AU agent (Prothrombinex-VF) contains factors II, IX, and X and works within minutes; vitamin K provides sustained factor synthesis as prothrombin complex clears. Fresh frozen plasma is second-line due to slow speed, large volume, and incomplete factor profile. In mechanical valve patients, recommencement of anticoagulation is balanced against rebleed risk; typically restart at 1 to 4 weeks based on imaging stability and valve type.
key_points: Major bleed on warfarin: prothrombin complex concentrate 50 IU/kg + IV vitamin K 10 mg
Prothrombinex-VF is the AU agent (3-factor: II, IX, X) - works within minutes
FFP is second-line: slow, volume burden, incomplete factor coverage
Mechanical mitral valve: restart anticoagulation at 1 to 4 weeks based on bleed stability
explanation.pearls: Prothrombinex-VF 50 IU/kg combined with intravenous vitamin K 10 mg restores warfarin-suppressed factors II, IX, and X within minutes while vitamin K secures sustained correction - the AU rapid-reversal pair for warfarin-associated intracerebral haemorrhage.
